plainMeaning: I bow to four more of the ever-living Jinas of the great central land — Vīrasena, Mahābhadra, Devayaśas and Ajitavīrya — and then to the eternal Jinas Ṛṣabha and Candrānana.
recitation: Vīrasena Mahābhadra vandūṁ, Devajasā dīṭhe ānandūṁ. Ajitavīriya vandana, śāśvatā Ṛṣabhā Candrānana.

wordByWord.gloss: Vīrasena — the 17th of the twenty living Jinas of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vīra (hero, brave) + senā (army) — 'one whose army is valour'.
wordByWord.notes: 17th in the standard list of viharamāṇa Tīrthaṅkaras.

wordByWord.gloss: Mahābhadra — the 18th living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit mahā (great) + bhadra (auspicious, good) — 'the greatly auspicious one'.
Old Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa 1st-person sg. from Sanskrit √vand (to salute, praise); the -ūṁ ending marks 'I … (do)'.

wordByWord.gloss: Devayaśas (Devajasā) — the 19th living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa for Sanskrit deva-yaśas: deva (god, divine) + yaśas (glory) — 'one of divine glory'.
wordByWord.notes: Printed Devajasā; equals the canonical Devyaśa/Devayaśas (#19).

wordByWord.gloss: on being beheld, when seen (i.e. at his darśana)
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati past participle dīṭha (seen) from Sanskrit dṛṣṭa (√dṛś, to see), locative-absolute sense 'when seen'.
wordByWord.notes: Conveys the joy of darśana; reads naturally with the following verb.

wordByWord.gloss: I rejoice, I am filled with bliss
wordByWord.etymology: Denominative Old Gujarati 1st-person sg. from Sanskrit ānanda (bliss, joy); -ūṁ = 'I …'.

wordByWord.gloss: Ajitavīrya — the 20th and last living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit a-jita (unconquered) + vīrya (energy, valour) — 'of unconquered energy'; -vīriya is the Prakrit form of vīrya.
wordByWord.notes: Completes the set of twenty viharamāṇa Jinas.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vandana (act of homage), from √vand; used predicatively, 'salutation (be) to (Ajitavīrya)'.
wordByWord.notes: Verbal-nominal usage parallel to vandūṁ in line 1.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit śāśvata (perpetual, eternal), from śaśvat (always); plural agreeing with the eternal Jina-names that follow.
wordByWord.notes: Signals the shift from living (viharamāṇa) to eternal (śāśvata) Jinas.

wordByWord.gloss: Ṛṣabha — the first of the four eternal Jina-names
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ṛṣabha (bull, the best/foremost) — the perennial 'foremost' Jina-name; final -ā is metrical lengthening.
wordByWord.notes: One of the four śāśvata names borne by eternal images everywhere.

wordByWord.gloss: Candrānana — the second of the four eternal Jina-names
wordByWord.notes: Distinct from the living Candrānana of v.3; here named as an eternal (śāśvata) Jina.

literalTranslation: Vīrasena and Mahābhadra I venerate; beholding Devayaśas, I rejoice. To Ajitavīrya, obeisance; (and to) the eternal ones, Ṛṣabha and Candrānana.
idiomaticTranslation: I bow before Vīrasena and Mahābhadra, and my heart fills with joy at the very sight of Devayaśas. To Ajitavīrya I offer my homage — and then to the ever-abiding Jinas, Ṛṣabha and Candrānana.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse does double duty. Its first three names — Vīrasena, Mahābhadra and Devayaśas — together with Ajitavīrya in the second line, are precisely the last four (17th–20th) of the twenty viharamāṇa Jinas presently teaching in Mahāvideha; the previous verse supplied numbers eleven through sixteen (Viśāladhara, Vajradhara, Candrānana, Candrabāhu, Bhujaṅga, Īśvara, Nemiprabha). With 'Ajitavīrya' the worshipper closes the living roll-call of twenty. The poet then pivots on a single word, śāśvatā, from the living Jinas to the eternal ones, naming the first two of the famous four perpetual Jina-names, Ṛṣabha and Candrānana (the next verse adds Vardhamāna and Vāriṣeṇa, rounding the set to a fourth twenty-four). Note the deliberately varied verbs of devotion in line one: not a flat list but vandūṁ ('I bow') answered by dīṭhe ānandūṁ ('beholding him, I rejoice') — the affective heart of a caitya-vandana, where mere seeing (darśana) blossoms into ānanda. The recurrence of the name Candrānana — earlier a living Jina, here an eternal one — is intentional, not a slip: the same auspicious names recur both among the viharamāṇas and among the śāśvatas, and the verse quietly trusts the reciter to feel that resonance rather than be confused by it.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse turns on the contrast between two kinds of Jina the tradition venerates side by side: viharamāṇa Jinas — actually embodied, omniscient teachers now wandering the Mahāvideha lands, so that the cosmos is never without a living guide — and śāśvata Jinas, the four eternal names (Ṛṣabha, Candrānana, Vāriṣeṇa, Vardhamāna) borne by the beginningless, ever-existing Jina-images enshrined throughout the three worlds. Bowing to both in one breath expresses a core Jain conviction: awakened reality is continuously present, both as living preceptors in time and as the timeless ideal that never arises or perishes. Completing the twenty living Jinas and opening the eternal set is also what carries this fourth roster toward twenty-four, the count that joins the three earlier sets into the ninety-six.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter is taught to respond, not merely enumerate: at each name, let 'dīṭhe ānandūṁ' become real — picture the Jina and let gladness rise at his darśana, even when no idol stands before you. Naming the ever-living Jinas of Mahāvideha reassures the practitioner that an awakened guide exists at this very moment; naming the eternal Jinas anchors faith in something beyond the flux of time, steadying samyaktva at dawn.

crossReferences.text: The four eternal (śāśvata) Jina-names — Ṛṣabha, Candrānana, Vāriṣeṇa, Vardhamāna — borne by eternal Jina-images

notes: Spellings are kept verbatim per the book: 'દેવજસા' (Devajasā) = the canonical Devyaśa/Devayaśas (#19); 'અજિતવીરિય' (Ajitavīriya) = Ajitavīrya (#20). The first-person verb endings -ūṁ (vandūṁ, ānandūṁ) are Old Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa; 'દીઠે' is read as a locative-absolute participle ('when/while seen'). The verse names only two of the four śāśvata Jinas (Ṛṣabha, Candrānana); the following verse completes them with Vardhamāna and Vāriṣeṇa. 'Candrānana' appears both as a living Jina (v.3) and here as an eternal name — an intentional recurrence in the tradition, not a textual error. The viharamāṇa-vs-śāśvata reading follows the hymn's own enumeration; individual viharamāṇa name-forms vary slightly across editions (e.g. Devyaśa/Devjas).
